era_context: The mid-8th century CE spans the early Abbasid Caliphate (established 750), the Tang Dynasty at its height in China, the Carolingian Renaissance under Pepin III and early Charlemagne in Western Europe, and the consolidation of Islamic rule across the Mediterranean and Central Asia. This is a pivotal moment of religious, political, and cultural transformation across Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources; archaeological record incomplete)
Oceania and Pacific islands (oral traditions not well-preserved in written form)
Northern Europe beyond Frankish territories (sparse contemporary documentation)

events.title: Battle of the Great Zab and fall of the Umayyad Caliphate
events.description: On 25 July 750 CE, Abbasid forces under Abu Muslim Khorasani defeated the last Umayyad caliph Marwan II at the Great Zab River. This decisive victory ended nearly a century of Umayyad rule (661–750) and inaugurated the Abbasid Caliphate, which would dominate the Islamic world for centuries. The battle marked a shift in the centre of Islamic power from Syria to Iraq and signalled the rise of Persian administrative influence within the caliphate. Marwan II fled to Egypt, where he was hunted down and killed in 750 or 751.

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (Tarikh al-Rusul wa-al-Muluk)

events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in multiple Islamic and Byzantine sources, though written down generations later. The date of 25 July 750 is given by al-Tabari and is accepted by modern scholarship. Marwan II's death is sometimes dated to 750 or 751; sources differ slightly.
cross_references: Relates to 751-001 (Battle of Talas, Abbasid expansion eastward)

events.title: Establishment of the Abbasid Caliphate
events.description: Following the victory at the Great Zab, Abu al-Abbas as-Saffah was proclaimed the first Abbasid caliph in Khorasan in 750 CE. The Abbasid revolution, which had been building for decades through underground networks (the Hashimiyya), now became the dominant Islamic state. The Abbasids promised a more inclusive, merit-based administration and claimed descent from Muhammad's uncle al-Abbas. This marked a fundamental shift from Umayyad Arab aristocracy to a more cosmopolitan, Persian-influenced bureaucratic model that would define Islamic governance for centuries.

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings
Al-Jahshiyari, Book of Ministers and Secretaries

events.certainty_note: The establishment of the Abbasid Caliphate in 750 is well-attested in Islamic historiography. The date is secure, though details of early administrative structure are reconstructed from later sources.

events.title: Tang Dynasty at peak territorial extent under Xuanzong
events.description: In 750 CE, the Tang Dynasty under Emperor Xuanzong (r. 712–756) was at or near its maximum territorial extent, controlling vast regions from Korea to Central Asia. The Tang had recently consolidated control over the Tarim Basin and maintained military garrisons along the Silk Road. However, this year marks the beginning of the end of Tang dominance: the An Lushan Rebellion would erupt in 755, and Xuanzong's reign would be remembered as the transition from Tang expansion to contraction. The court at Chang'an was a centre of cosmopolitan culture, attracting merchants, monks, and scholars from across Asia.
key_figures: An Lushan (general, not yet in rebellion)
Yang Guifei (imperial concubine, influential at court)

sources.name: Jiu Tangshu (Old History of Tang), compiled 945 CE
Xin Tangshu (New History of Tang), compiled 1060 CE

events.certainty_note: The Tang Dynasty's territorial extent and Xuanzong's reign are well-documented in Chinese historical records. The year 750 is a conventional marker; the transition from expansion to contraction was gradual.

events.title: Pepin III consolidates Frankish power; Donation of Pepin
events.region: Frankish kingdom (modern France, parts of Germany and Italy)
events.description: In 750 CE, Pepin III (the Short), mayor of the palace under the last Merovingian king Childeric III, moved to legitimise his rule. In 751, Pepin deposed Childeric and was crowned king by Pope Zachary, establishing the Carolingian dynasty. The events of 750–751 represent a crucial transition in Western European politics: the shift from Merovingian to Carolingian rule and the formalisation of the Frankish-papal alliance. Pepin's military campaigns against the Lombards and his subsequent 'Donation of Pepin' (756) would lay the foundation for the Papal States and the later Carolingian Empire.
key_figures: Pepin III (the Short, mayor of the palace, soon to be king)

sources.name: Einhard, Life of Charlemagne (Vita Karoli Magni)

events.certainty_note: The deposition of Childeric III and Pepin's rise are attested in multiple sources, but the exact date is sometimes given as 751 rather than 750. The sources are not contemporary; the Liber Pontificalis was compiled in the 9th century.
cross_references: Leads to 756-001 (Donation of Pepin to the papacy)

events.title: Iconoclasm in the Byzantine Empire under Constantine V
events.description: In 750 CE, the Byzantine Emperor Constantine V (r. 741–775) was actively enforcing iconoclasm—the destruction and prohibition of religious images. Constantine V had inherited the iconoclastic policy from his father Leo III and pursued it with vigour, convening the Council of Hieria in 754 to condemn icon veneration as idolatry. This period of intense iconoclasm (726–787) caused significant religious and social upheaval, leading to the destruction of countless religious artworks and the persecution of icon-venerating monks. The policy was deeply unpopular with the populace and the papacy, contributing to the growing estrangement between Eastern and Western Christianity.
key_figures: Leo III (Constantine's father, initiator of iconoclasm)

sources.name: John of Damascus, On the Divine Images (Apologia contra eos qui aboleverunt imagines)

events.certainty_note: Iconoclasm under Constantine V is well-attested in Byzantine and Western sources. The Council of Hieria is dated to 754, and Constantine's policies in 750 are documented in later chronicles.
cross_references: Relates to 787-001 (Second Council of Nicaea, restoration of icons)

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of 750 CE as a 'turning point' is somewhat historiographically constructed. While the Battle of the Great Zab and the fall of the Umayyads are confirmed events, the notion that the Abbasid Caliphate immediately represented a fundamental improvement in governance or inclusivity is disputed by modern scholars. Some argue that the Abbasid revolution was primarily a power transfer among elites, with limited immediate benefit to non-Arab Muslims. Additionally, the dating of Pepin III's deposition of Childeric III is sometimes placed in 751 rather than 750, and the 'Donation of Pepin' is a later papal construction; the actual extent of Pepin's territorial grants to the papacy is debated. Constantine V's iconoclasm is confirmed, but its characterisation as uniquely destructive or unpopular is partly shaped by later pro-icon sources written by his opponents.
historiographic_note: The year 750 CE is well-documented in Islamic, Chinese, and Western European sources, though most surviving accounts were written generations or centuries later. Islamic historiography is dominated by al-Tabari and later compilers, who drew on earlier (now lost) sources; this creates a risk of narrative distortion. Chinese sources are abundant but court-sponsored and may downplay internal instability. Western European sources are sparse and often hagiographic. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Indian subcontinent, and Oceania are largely absent from contemporary documentation for this year. The 'significance' of 750 is partly a retrospective construction: the Abbasid victory was momentous, but its long-term consequences were not immediately apparent.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of the Great Zab directly enabled the establishment of the Abbasid Caliphate
Tang territorial extent and Xuanzong's reign set the stage for the An Lushan Rebellion
Pepin III's consolidation of power in 750 led to his formal coronation as king in 751
Constantine V's iconoclasm provoked a reaction that culminated in the restoration of icons at the Second Council of Nicaea in 787
